issues.description: All controllers use console.log(error) for error handling instead of structured logging. This is the standard AutoBE pattern but lacks severity levels, request context, and correlation IDs needed for production monitoring.
issues.suggestion: Replace console.log with a structured logger (e.g., NestJS Logger or Winston) that includes request context, error stack traces, and severity levels. Apply consistently across all controllers.

issues.description: Image URLs submitted in the request body are stored without validation of the URL scheme or domain. A malicious seller could store javascript: URIs or internal network URLs that could be rendered by client applications.
issues.suggestion: Validate that image URLs use https:// scheme and optionally restrict to an allowlist of trusted CDN domains before storing them.

issues.description: Rate limiting is not applied to any endpoint in this codebase. High-privilege endpoints like force-cancel and force-refund are particularly sensitive to abuse.
issues.suggestion: Apply rate limiting at the infrastructure level (API gateway, reverse proxy) or use @nestjs/throttler for endpoint-level rate limiting, especially on sensitive admin operations.

issues.description: Controllers use console.log(error) for error handling across all files in this chunk. This is the standard AutoBE pattern but does not provide structured logging with request context, severity levels, or correlation IDs.
issues.suggestion: Consider integrating a structured logging library (e.g., Winston, Pino) with request correlation IDs for production observability. Apply consistently across all controllers and providers.

issues.description: The `index` (PATCH) and `at` (GET) endpoints for product variant options are explicitly marked as public (x-autobe-authorization-type: null) and accessible to unauthenticated guests. This is intentional per the documentation for public product catalog browsing.
issues.suggestion: Confirm this is intentional. If any option data could be sensitive (e.g., internal SKU codes), consider requiring at least a soft authentication check.

issues.description: When promoting an admin to super admin, the password_hash from the admin record is directly copied to the new super admin record without re-hashing or requiring password re-confirmation. If the admin's password hash was compromised or uses a weaker algorithm, it is silently promoted to super admin level.
issues.suggestion: Require explicit password confirmation during promotion, or at minimum verify the hash algorithm meets current security standards before copying. Consider forcing a password reset after promotion.

issues.description: The order status recalculation logic uses a plain string-keyed Record for status priority mapping without exhaustive validation. If an unexpected status value appears in the database (e.g., due to a migration or bug), the fallback `statusPriority[s] ?? -1` silently treats it as the lowest priority, potentially setting an incorrect order status.
issues.suggestion: Use an explicit enum or exhaustive switch statement for order status transitions. Add a validation step that throws if an unrecognized status is encountered, rather than silently defaulting.

issues.description: No rate limiting is applied to the super admin customer search endpoint. A compromised super admin token could be used to enumerate all customer records rapidly.
issues.suggestion: Apply rate limiting at the infrastructure or middleware level for all admin search/list endpoints.

issues.description: Profile update checks if the customer is banned but does not check if the customer is suspended (if such a state exists). The ban check is good practice, but ensure all relevant account state flags are validated before allowing mutations.
issues.suggestion: Review all account state flags (banned, suspended, deleted) and ensure profile mutation endpoints validate all relevant states consistently.

issues.description: Inventory aggregation uses `productVariant: { id: existing.product_variant_id }` as a relation filter. Depending on Prisma schema configuration, this may not be the most direct query. If the relation name is incorrect or the query is inefficient, it could lead to incorrect stock calculations.
issues.suggestion: Verify the Prisma relation filter matches the schema definition. Consider using `shopping_mall_product_variant_id: existing.product_variant_id` as a direct field filter for clarity and correctness.

issues.description: The function returns `typia.random<IShoppingMallSellerApproval>()` — randomly generated fake data — as a real API response to an authenticated admin request. This exposes fabricated sensitive data (seller approval records) to callers and masks a missing implementation, potentially misleading consumers about system state.
issues.suggestion: Throw a proper 501 Not Implemented or 404 HttpException instead of returning random data. Never use typia.random() in production response paths.

issues.description: The force-refund endpoint accepts `orderItemIds` from the request body and validates they belong to the order, but there is no check that the order itself belongs to the admin's scope or tenant. Any admin can force-refund any order by ID.
issues.suggestion: If multi-tenancy or scoped admin access is required, add a check that the order belongs to the admin's allowed scope before processing.

issues.description: Order item ownership and status are validated in a loop outside the transaction. Between the validation loop and the transaction execution, another concurrent request could change item status or assignment (TOCTOU race condition), allowing double-assignment of order items to shipments.
issues.suggestion: Move all validation queries (ownership, status, shipmentItem checks) inside the $transaction block, or use SELECT FOR UPDATE / Prisma's atomic operations to prevent race conditions.

issues.description: Stock floor check (step 5) is performed outside the transaction that creates the inventory record. A concurrent negative adjustment could pass the floor check simultaneously, resulting in stock going below zero.
issues.suggestion: Move the aggregate stock check inside the $transaction block alongside the record creation to ensure atomicity of the check-then-write operation.

issues.description: No audit logging is performed for the force-cancel operation. Administrative actions that modify order state and restore inventory should be logged for accountability and forensic purposes.
issues.suggestion: Add structured audit log entries for force-cancel operations, recording the acting super admin ID, order ID, affected items, and timestamp.

issues.description: No rate limiting is applied to this search/listing endpoint. Customers could enumerate refund request data at high frequency.
issues.suggestion: Apply rate limiting at the infrastructure or middleware level for all customer-facing endpoints.

agentEvaluations.summary: [sampled 30/291 files] [3 chunks, 16→16 issues] The codebase demonstrates good security practices overall. All sensitive endpoints use appropriate authentication decorators (SellerAuth, AdminAuth, SuperadminAuth), UUID format validation is enforced on path parameters via typia tags, and ownership checks are documented in the provider specifications. The main concerns are: image URL validation is absent at the controller/provider level (potential for storing malicious URLs), and console.log error handling lacks structured logging. No injection vulnerabilities, broken access control bypasses, or cryptographic issues are evident in the controller layer. The AutoBE-standard patterns (console.log, no rate limiting) are noted as suggestions only.
